the problem asks us to find the denominator of the fraction $\frac{100!}{10^{50}}$ when it is written in its irreducible form. to approach this, we need to understand how to simplify such a fraction. first, we recognize that $10^{50}$ can be written as $2^{50} \times 5^{50}$, since $10 = 2 \times 5$. this is important because the prime factors of $10^{50}$ are only 2 and 5.

*** 

next, we need to consider the prime factorization of $100!$. the factorial of 100 includes all integers from 1 to 100, so its prime factorization will contain many factors of 2 and 5, as well as other primes. to simplify the fraction, we must divide the numerator and the denominator by their greatest common divisor (gcd). the gcd of $100!$ and $10^{50}$ will be determined by the minimum of the exponents of the common prime factors in both numbers.

*** 

to find the exponent of 2 in $100!$, we use the formula that sums the integer divisions of 100 by powers of 2. this gives us the total number of times 2 appears in the prime factorization of $100!$. similarly, we calculate the exponent of 5 in $100!$ by summing the integer divisions of 100 by powers of 5. these exponents will help us determine how many factors of 2 and 5 we can cancel out from the denominator $10^{50}$.

*** 

after determining the exponents of 2 and 5 in $100!$, we subtract the exponents from the exponents in $10^{50}$. this will give us the remaining exponents of 2 and 5 in the simplified fraction. since the denominator must be a product of primes, and we have already canceled out the common factors, the remaining factors in the denominator will be the ones that were not canceled out. these will be the primes other than 2 and 5 that are still present in the denominator.

***
